resources.string: How long should you wash your hands with soap to effectively remove germs?
The WHO recommends washing hands with soap for at least 20 seconds to effectively remove germs and prevent disease transmission.
Which of these is NOT one of the critical moments for handwashing?
Critical moments for handwashing include before preparing/eating food, after using the toilet, after coughing/sneezing, and after touching animals. Watching television is not a critical moment.
What is the most effective way to make water safe for drinking in a household?
Boiling it for at least 1 minute
Boiling water for at least 1 minute (3 minutes at high altitude) kills most disease-causing organisms and is the most reliable household method for making water safe.
What is the correct way to dispose of a child\'s feces?
Dispose of it in a latrine or toilet
A child\'s feces should always be disposed of in a latrine or toilet. Open defecation contaminates water sources and spreads diseases like cholera and typhoid.
Which disease is most commonly spread through contaminated water?
Cholera is primarily spread through contaminated water and food. It causes severe diarrhea and dehydration and can be fatal if untreated.
What is the recommended duration for exclusive breastfeeding?
The WHO recommends exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months of life. After 6 months, complementary foods should be introduced while continuing breastfeeding up to 2 years or beyond.
Which nutrient deficiency causes night blindness in children?
Vitamin A deficiency is the leading cause of preventable childhood blindness. Foods rich in Vitamin A include orange and yellow fruits/vegetables, dark leafy greens, liver, and eggs.
What is a sign of severe acute malnutrition in children?
Bilateral pitting edema (swelling in both feet) is a sign of kwashiorkor, a form of severe acute malnutrition. Other signs include wasting, very low MUAC, and visible severe wasting.
At what age should complementary foods be introduced to a breastfed infant?
Complementary foods should be introduced at 6 months of age while continuing breastfeeding. Before 6 months, breast milk alone provides all the nutrients a baby needs.
Which of these is the best source of iron for preventing anemia?
Dark leafy greens (such as spinach and kale) and organ meats like liver are excellent sources of iron. Iron deficiency anemia is one of the most common nutritional deficiencies worldwide.
How many antenatal care visits does the WHO recommend during pregnancy?
The WHO recommends at least 8 antenatal care contacts during pregnancy to reduce perinatal mortality and improve women\'s experience of care.
Which of these is a danger sign during pregnancy that requires immediate medical attention?

Vaginal bleeding during pregnancy is a danger sign that could indicate placenta previa, placental abruption, or miscarriage. Other danger signs include severe headache, blurred vision, convulsions, and high fever.
What supplement should pregnant women take to prevent neural tube defects?
Folic acid (folate) supplementation before and during early pregnancy significantly reduces the risk of neural tube defects like spina bifida in newborns.
What is the leading cause of maternal death globally?
Postpartum hemorrhage (severe bleeding after childbirth) is the leading cause of maternal death worldwide. Active management of the third stage of labor helps prevent it.
When should a newborn be put to the breast for the first time?
Within the first hour after birth
Early initiation of breastfeeding within the first hour of birth is crucial. Colostrum (first milk) is rich in antibodies and nutrients essential for the newborn\'s immune system.
At what age should a child receive the BCG vaccine?
The BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guerin) vaccine against tuberculosis should be given at birth or as soon as possible after birth.
What does the pentavalent vaccine protect against?
Malaria, TB, cholera, typhoid, and polio
Diphtheria, pertussis, tetanus, hepatitis B, and Hib
Measles, mumps, rubella, chickenpox, and polio
HIV, hepatitis A, hepatitis B, HPV, and meningitis
The pentavalent vaccine protects against 5 diseases: Diphtheria, Pertussis (whooping cough), Tetanus, Hepatitis B, and Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib).
At what age is the measles vaccine typically given?
The first dose of measles vaccine is typically given at 9 months of age. A second dose is given at 18 months in many countries to ensure full protection.
What is the correct way to store most vaccines?
Most vaccines must be stored in a refrigerator at 2-8°C to maintain their effectiveness. This is known as the cold chain. Some vaccines must never be frozen.
Which of these is a contraindication for vaccination?
Previous severe allergic reaction to the vaccine
A previous severe allergic reaction (anaphylaxis) to a vaccine or its components is a true contraindication. Mild illness, diarrhea, or being underweight are NOT reasons to delay vaccination.
What is the main way malaria is transmitted?
Malaria is transmitted through the bite of an infected female Anopheles mosquito. Prevention includes sleeping under insecticide-treated nets and removing standing water.
Which of these is a symptom of tuberculosis (TB)?
Persistent cough lasting more than 2 weeks
A persistent cough lasting more than 2 weeks is the most common symptom of pulmonary TB. Other symptoms include night sweats, weight loss, fever, and coughing up blood.
What is the most effective way to prevent mosquito-borne diseases?
Sleeping under insecticide-treated bed nets (ITNs) is one of the most effective ways to prevent malaria and other mosquito-borne diseases. Nets should be retreated or replaced regularly.
What is the oral rehydration solution (ORS) used for?
ORS is a mixture of clean water, salt, and sugar used to prevent and treat dehydration caused by diarrhea. It replaces lost fluids and electrolytes and saves millions of lives annually.
Through unprotected sexual contact and contaminated blood
HIV is primarily transmitted through unprotected sexual contact, contaminated blood (needles/transfusions), and from mother to child during pregnancy, birth, or breastfeeding.
What is the first step when encountering an unconscious person?
Check for responsiveness and call for help
Wait for them to wake up
The first step is to check for responsiveness by tapping and shouting, then call for help/emergency services. Check for breathing and begin CPR if needed.
What is the correct compression rate for adult CPR?
The correct compression rate for CPR is 100-120 compressions per minute. Compressions should be at least 2 inches (5 cm) deep for adults.
How should you treat a minor burn?
Cool with clean running water for at least 20 minutes
Minor burns should be cooled with clean running water for at least 20 minutes. Do not apply butter, oil, toothpaste, or ice. Cover with a clean, non-stick dressing.
What should you do if someone is choking and cannot cough, speak, or breathe?
For a conscious choking adult, perform abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver) by standing behind them and giving upward thrusts to the abdomen above the navel.
How should you control severe bleeding from a wound?
Remove the bandage frequently to check
Apply firm, direct pressure with a clean cloth
Apply a tourniquet as the first step
Apply firm, direct pressure to the wound using a clean cloth or dressing. Maintain pressure continuously. Elevate the injured limb if possible. Only use a tourniquet as a last resort.
What is a sign of pneumonia in a child under 5?

Fast breathing or difficulty breathing is a key sign of pneumonia in children under 5. Other signs include chest in-drawing, inability to feed, and fever. Pneumonia is a leading killer of children under 5.
What is the danger sign of dehydration in infants?
A sunken fontanelle (soft spot on the baby\'s head) is a sign of dehydration. Other signs include dry mouth, no tears when crying, decreased urination, and lethargy.
At what age should a child start receiving vitamin A supplementation?
Vitamin A supplementation should begin at 6 months of age and continue every 6 months until age 5. Vitamin A is crucial for immune function and preventing blindness.
What is the MUAC cutoff for severe acute malnutrition in children 6-59 months?
A MUAC less than 11.5 cm indicates severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in children aged 6-59 months. MUAC between 11.5-12.5 cm indicates moderate acute malnutrition (MAM).
How should oral rehydration solution (ORS) be prepared?
Mix the entire packet with 500 ml of water
Mix the entire packet with 1 liter of clean water
Mix half the packet with 2 liters of water
One ORS packet should be dissolved in 1 liter (1000 ml) of clean water. The solution should be used within 24 hours. Do not boil the solution or add sugar.
What is the purpose of a community health worker\'s home visit?
To assess health, provide education, and refer when needed
Community health workers conduct home visits to assess the health of family members, provide health education, identify danger signs, make referrals, and follow up on treatments.
What does the term \'referral\' mean in community health?
Directing a patient to a higher-level health facility for additional care
A referral means directing a patient to a higher-level health facility or specialist for care that cannot be provided at the community level. Timely referrals can save lives.
Which of these is an important skill for a Community Health Volunteer?
Active listening and health education are essential skills for Community Health Volunteers. CHVs serve as the link between the community and the health system.
What is the purpose of growth monitoring in children?
To measure how tall a child will become as an adult
To track a child\'s growth and detect malnutrition early
To decide what school they should attend
Growth monitoring tracks a child\'s weight and height over time to detect growth faltering or malnutrition early. This allows for timely intervention and nutritional support.
What should a CHV do when they identify a danger sign in a patient?
Wait and observe for a week
Refer the patient immediately to a health facility
Ignore it if the patient feels fine
When a CHV identifies danger signs, they should refer the patient immediately to the nearest health facility. Delay in referral can lead to complications or death.
Deciding how many rooms a house should have
The ability to decide the number and spacing of children using contraceptive methods
Family planning allows individuals and couples to decide the number and spacing of their children through the use of contraceptive methods and information.
Which of these is a long-acting reversible contraceptive method?
An IUD (intrauterine device) is a long-acting reversible contraceptive that can last 5-12 years depending on the type. Other long-acting methods include implants.
What is the most effective way to prevent sexually transmitted infections (STIs)?
Correct and consistent use of condoms
Correct and consistent use of condoms is the most effective way to prevent STIs besides abstinence. Birth control pills prevent pregnancy but do not protect against STIs.
What is the recovery position used for?
Placing an unconscious but breathing person on their side to keep their airway clear
The recovery position is used for unconscious people who are breathing normally. It keeps the airway clear and prevents choking on vomit or fluids.
What are the signs of a stroke?
Face drooping, arm weakness, speech difficulty
Remember FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency services. Stroke requires immediate medical attention to prevent brain damage.
What should you do if someone is having a seizure?
Clear the area of hard objects and protect their head
During a seizure, clear the area of hard or sharp objects, protect the person\'s head, and do NOT put anything in their mouth or restrain them. Turn them on their side after the seizure stops.
